Beyond oil and gas, the method is employed in mining for precious metals, geothermal reservoir temperature estimation, and carbon sequestration. During the late 19th century, the Italian Regia Marina sought to modernize its fleet by developing a series of protected cruisers. Prior to this era, Italian naval architecture relied heavily on foreign expertise, with most ships of this type being designed in Britain or based on British models like the Etna class. This status changed with the development of the Regioni class, which consisted of six vessels: Umbria, Lombardia, Etruria, Liguria, Elba, and Puglia. These ships were utilized in diverse capacities, from serving as scouts and colonial representatives to being converted into specialized vessels like seaplane tenders and submarine depot ships. Despite their varied service histories, notably during the Italo-Turkish War and World War I, the class was generally considered a disappointment due to insufficient armor and underwhelming speed. Unlike many other contemporary settlements that utilized hilltop positions, Manching relied on its strategic location at the intersection of vital trade routes and its proximity to rich deposits of gold and iron ore. The site reached its peak size of 380 hectares in the 2nd century BC, protected by a massive 7.2-kilometer-long circuit of walls. Headquartered in Hopkinton, Massachusetts, the company experienced a massive transformation in the 1990s, evolving from a firm valued in the hundreds of millions to a multi-billion dollar enterprise. This rapid scaling was largely driven by the leadership of CEO Michael Ruettgers and the commercial success of the company's flagship storage platform. One such species is a free-living ground dweller that inhabits the Fynbos, Grassland, and Savanna biomes, ranging from sea level up to elevations of 1,816 meters. While typically found on the ground, specimens have been collected at heights of 6 meters via canopy fogging of Pappea capensis trees, suggesting occasional arboreal behavior. During the French and Indian War, a British expedition led by General John Forbes set out in July 1758 with 6,000 troops to seize control of the Ohio Country. As the army advanced slowly toward its objective, Forbes fell ill and delegated command to his Swiss subordinate, Lt. Col. Henry Bouquet. In late August, Bouquet authorized a reconnaissance mission of 850 men led by Major James Grant of the 77th Regiment. Grant underestimated the enemy strength, believing the garrison consisted of only 200 men rather than the actual force of hundreds of French soldiers and their native allies led by François-Marie Le Marchand de Lignery. On the morning of September 14, Grant attempted to lure the defenders into an ambush by sending a decoy party forward with drums beating and bagpipes playing. However, the French and Indians moved swiftly to surround the British, utilizing the forest cover and high ground to inflict heavy casualties. Although the British were defeated and Grant was captured, the French eventually realized they could not withstand the main army's superior numbers. Established in the early 18th century near St. Patrick's Cathedral in Dublin, Marsh's Library remains a remarkably preserved monument to the late Renaissance and early Enlightenment. Founded by Narcissus Marsh, the former Provost of Trinity College and later Archbishop of Dublin, the institution was designed by Sir William Robinson and formally incorporated by an act of the Irish Parliament in 1707. This legislative foundation vested the property and its vast collection—which includes over 25,000 books and 300 manuscripts—in a governing body known as the Governors and Guardians. 

The library's holdings are diverse, featuring significant collections from Marsh himself, as well as the personal library of the first librarian, a Huguenot refugee named Elias Bouhereau. The interior of the building maintains its original character, with Baltic oak bookcases that still bear bullet holes from the 1916 Easter Rising. Established in 1965 as the Federation of Debate Clubs, the Revolutionary Youth Federation of Turkey emerged as a prominent Marxist-Leninist organization during a period of intense political upheaval. Commonly referred to by its shortened name, the group became a focal point for radical student activism and militant opposition to foreign influence in Turkey. Throughout the late 1960s, its members were involved in high-profile acts of defiance, such as the 1969 incident where they set fire to the car of U.S. Ambassador Robert Komer during a university visit, and their active participation in protests against the U.S. Sixth Fleet. The organization also played a significant role in the labor movement, specifically during the worker actions of June 1970. Despite its influence, the group faced severe state repression; following the 1971 military coup, it was banned and its members were subjected to mass trials in military courts. The internal security of the movement was further compromised when CIA agent Aldrich Ames exposed the identities of many participants. Within the cultural traditions of the Khiamniungan people in Nagaland, specific regalia are reserved for use during the folk dances performed at annual tribal and village festivals. Among these items is a distinctive conical headpiece characterized by its intricate construction and deep symbolic value. Historically, these items were not only used locally but were also crafted alongside leggings known as Hokthie and gauntlets for trade with neighboring communities. The construction of an authentic piece is a complex process requiring more than twelve different materials sourced from the wild, each carrying its own historical significance. The frame is typically made of fine cane, often dyed red, and embellished with red goat hair, bear hair, and hornbill feathers. Some versions are further decorated with transparent flat horns made from mithan horn, while a chain of tiger claws often hangs around the chin. Collectively, these wild-sourced materials, including wild boar teeth, serve as a visual testament to the wearer's wealth, bravery, and victory over enemies.
